content: Georgia's next primary election is Tuesday, May 19, 2026. Polls are open 7:00 AM to 7:00 PM at your assigned polling place. A primary is how each political party picks its candidates for the November general election — so you're not picking who gets the job yet, you're picking which Democrats and Republicans advance. There are also some nonpartisan races on the ballot, like judges and school board, which are decided right then.
hypothetical_questions: When is the next election in Macon-Bibb?
When is the May 2026 primary?
What time do polls open on election day?
What time do polls close in Macon?
What is a primary election?
source: Georgia Secretary of State 2026 Election Calendar

content: At the May 19, 2026 primary, you have to pick one of three ballots: Democratic, Republican, or nonpartisan-only. Georgia doesn't ask you to register with a party — you just decide which ballot you want on the day you vote, and you can pick differently next time. The nonpartisan races (like judges and school board) are on every ballot no matter which one you pick.
hypothetical_questions: Do I have to pick a party to vote in the primary?
Can I vote in both primaries?
What is a nonpartisan ballot?
How does Georgia's open primary work?
Do I have to be a Democrat or Republican to vote?

content: If nobody gets more than 50% of the votes in a race, the top two candidates go to a runoff election — a second, smaller election just between them. The runoff is scheduled for June 16, 2026. If you were registered in time for the May primary, you're automatically registered for the runoff too.
hypothetical_questions: When is the primary runoff?
What happens if no candidate wins 50%?
Do I need to re-register for the runoff?
When is the runoff election?
What is a runoff?
source: Georgia Secretary of State 2026 Election Calendar

content: The Georgia governor's race is the top contest on the May 19, 2026 primary ballot. It is an open seat with no incumbent running, and both Democratic and Republican primaries are heavily contested. The governor serves a four-year term as Georgia's chief executive and sets policy that directly affects Macon-Bibb County services, education, and infrastructure.

content: Seven Democrats qualified for the May 19, 2026 governor primary: Amanda Duffy, Derrick Jackson, Geoff Duncan, Jason Esteves, Keisha Lance Bottoms, Michael Thurmond, and Olu Brown. Notable figures include Keisha Lance Bottoms (former Atlanta mayor), Michael Thurmond (DeKalb County CEO), Geoff Duncan (former Republican lieutenant governor), and Jason Esteves (state senator). A runoff will occur on June 16 if no Democrat clears 50%.

Williams. Notable figures include Burt Jones (current lieutenant governor), Chris Carr (current attorney general), and Brad Raffensperger (current secretary of state). A runoff will occur on June 16 if no Republican clears 50%.

content: U.S. senators serve six-year terms and represent Georgia in Congress, voting on federal legislation, judicial confirmations, and federal funding that affects Macon-Bibb County. Georgia's other senator, Raphael Warnock, is not up for election in 2026. Only Jon Ossoff's seat is on this year's ballot.

content: The Georgia lieutenant governor's race is an open seat on the May 19, 2026 primary because incumbent Burt Jones is running for governor. content: Bibb County is split between two U.S. House districts following the court-ordered 2023 redistricting: the 2nd Congressional District and the 12th Congressional District. Which district a Macon-Bibb voter is in depends on their specific address within the county. Voters can verify their assigned district at mvp.sos.ga.gov.

content: In U.S. House District 2, which covers the southern and western portions of Bibb County, Democratic incumbent Sanford Bishop Jr. is unopposed in his primary and Republican Benjamin Day is unopposed in his primary. Rep. Bishop has served Georgia's 2nd District since 1993 and is a senior member of the House Appropriations Committee, giving him significant influence over federal funding for Middle Georgia.

content: Supreme Court justices serve six-year terms and have final authority on constitutional questions and appeals in Georgia, including cases involving voting rights, criminal justice, and property law. Georgia Supreme Court races are nonpartisan, meaning candidates do not run with party labels and the contest appears on every ballot.

content: Bibb County school board members set policy and approve budgets for the Bibb County School District, which serves approximately 21,000 students across 35 or more schools. Board members serve four-year terms in nonpartisan elections, with at-large posts elected countywide. The district superintendent is Dr. Dan A. Sims, who has served since July 2022.

content: Four Macon Water Authority races appear on the May 19, 2026 nonpartisan ballot: Chairman (countywide), a special election for District 2, District 4, and District 1. The Macon Water Authority is a seven-member board with five single-member district seats and two countywide seats that oversees drinking water, wastewater, and stormwater systems for Macon-Bibb County. These races directly affect water quality and utility rates for every household.

content: The Macon Water Authority District 2 seat is a special election on the May 19, 2026 ballot because incumbent Desmond Brown resigned from the seat on March 6, 2026 to qualify for the Chairman's race. Under the authority's charter, when a vacancy will last more than 120 days, it must be filled at the next general election rather than by appointment. This special election was added to the ballot on March 20, 2026 after Chairman Gary Bechtel requested the Board of Elections include it.
hypothetical_questions: Why is there a Water Authority special election?
What is the District 2 special election?
Why did Desmond Brown resign?

content: Four candidates are running in the May 19, 2026 special election for Macon Water Authority District 2: Marshall Talley (hospitality business owner), Ronald Edward Lemon (chiropractor), Sharif Robbins-Brinson (public administration graduate student and local Democratic Party officer), and Renoalda Scott (property manager, tax preparer, and chair of the Macon-Bibb County Democratic Committee). District 2 covers parts of East Macon west of Gray Highway, downtown, Pleasant Hill, Vineville, Ingleside, Unionville, Payne City, Napier Heights, Bloomfield, and south Bibb County from Eisenhower Parkway to Rocky Creek Road. The district has approximately 29,000 registered voters.

content: The Macon Water Authority Chairman's race on the May 19, 2026 ballot is a countywide nonpartisan contest between incumbent Gary Bechtel and challenger Desmond Brown. Bechtel, 65, was appointed to the board in 2017-18 and elected chair in 2022, defeating Brown in a June 2022 runoff by 8,519 to 3,826 votes. A third candidate, Donald Druitt, qualified but has dropped out of the race.

content: Three candidates are running for Macon Water Authority District 4 on May 19, 2026: 22-year incumbent Frank Patterson (age 78, retired from Bibb County public works and engineering), De'Ron Rogers (age 62, owner of Pinky's Shaved Ice and a 2024 candidate for State House District 145), and Michael McKeever (age 32, software program manager at Robins Air Force Base). District 4 covers southwest Bibb County, Lizella, Lake Tobesofkee, the Macon Mall, Rutland, and a slice of north Macon, with approximately 29,000 registered voters. The board is currently seeking a new CEO following the February 2026 death of longtime CEO Ron Shipman.

content: To vote in the May 19, 2026 Georgia primary, you need to be registered by Monday, April 20, 2026. The easiest way is online at registertovote.sos.ga.gov — you just need a Georgia driver's license or state ID. You can also register in person at the Bibb County Board of Elections (3661 Eisenhower Parkway, inside the Macon Mall) or by mail. To register you need to be 18 by Election Day, a U.S. citizen, and living in Bibb County.
hypothetical_questions: When is the voter registration deadline?
How do I register to vote in Macon?
Can I register to vote online in Georgia?
Where do I register to vote in Bibb County?
How do I sign up to vote?

content: Early voting for the May 19, 2026 primary runs from Monday, April 27 through Friday, May 15. That's almost three weeks of chances to vote before Election Day. Early voting is also open on two Saturdays: May 2 and May 9. Sunday voting may also happen on May 3 and May 10 depending on what the county decides.
hypothetical_questions: When does early voting start?
When does early voting end?
Can I vote early on Saturday?
What are the early voting dates?
Is there Sunday voting?
Can I vote before May 19?
source: Georgia Secretary of State 2026 Election Calendar

content: During early voting, you can vote at the Board of Elections office at 3661 Eisenhower Parkway, inside the Macon Mall. Weekday hours are usually 8:30 AM to 5:30 PM. Saturday hours are usually 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM. The county may open more early voting sites too — check maconbibb.us/board-of-elections or call (478) 621-6622 to see the full list before you go.
hypothetical_questions: Where can I vote early?
What are the early voting hours?
Where is the Board of Elections office?
What time can I vote early?

content: If you want to vote by mail, you need to request your absentee ballot by Friday, May 8, 2026. The easiest way is online at securemyabsenteeballot.sos.ga.gov. You can also request by mail, email (boe@maconbibb.us), fax, or in person. Georgia doesn't require a reason — anyone can vote absentee. You'll need your driver's license or state ID number to request.
hypothetical_questions: When is the absentee ballot deadline?
How do I request an absentee ballot?
Can I vote by mail in Georgia?
Can I vote absentee?
How do I vote from home?

content: Your completed absentee ballot has to get back to the Bibb County Board of Elections by 7:00 PM on Election Day, May 19, 2026. You can mail it in, drop it off in person at the office (3661 Eisenhower Parkway), or put it in the secure drop box inside the office during early voting hours. You can track your ballot's status at mvp.sos.ga.gov to make sure it was received and counted.
hypothetical_questions: When is the absentee ballot due?
Where do I return my absentee ballot?
Can I use a drop box?
How do I track my absentee ballot?
When does my absentee ballot have to be back?

content: On Election Day, May 19, 2026, you vote at your assigned precinct — a specific polling place based on where you live. (That's different from early voting, where you can vote anywhere in the county.) Find your precinct at mvp.sos.ga.gov, at maconbibb.us/board-of-elections/where-to-vote, or call (478) 621-6622. Polls are open 7:00 AM to 7:00 PM.
hypothetical_questions: Where do I vote on Election Day?
Where is my polling place?

How do I find my precinct?
Where do I go to vote?

content: The Bibb County Board of Elections is located at 3661 Eisenhower Parkway, Suite MB101, Macon, GA 31206, inside the Macon Mall. The office can be reached at (478) 621-6622, fax (478) 621-6119, or email boe@maconbibb.us, open Monday through Friday 8:30 AM to 5:30 PM. The elections supervisor is Tom Gillon (tgillon@maconbibb.us), and the website is maconbibb.us/board-of-elections.

content: To check voter registration, find a polling location, or view a personalized sample ballot for the May 19, 2026 primary, voters can visit the Georgia Secretary of State's My Voter Page at mvp.sos.ga.gov. The tool shows assigned congressional, state legislative, and county commission districts and displays only the races on that specific voter's ballot. The Macon-Bibb Board of Elections also posts a candidate list PDF at maconbibb.us/board-of-elections.
hypothetical_questions: How do I check my voter registration?

content: Key dates for the May 19, 2026 primary in Macon-Bibb County: voter registration deadline April 20, early voting April 27 through May 15, absentee ballot request deadline May 8, Election Day May 19 with polls open 7:00 AM to 7:00 PM. If any race goes to a runoff, it will be held June 16, 2026, and voters registered by the April 20 deadline are automatically registered for the runoff. The general election follows November 3, 2026.

content: Lester Miller is the current Mayor of Macon-Bibb County, serving his second and final four-year term. He was first elected in 2019, took office January 2020, and was re-elected in May 2024. His current term runs through January 2029. Miller is the second mayor of the consolidated government, succeeding Robert Reichert, who was elected the first mayor in September 2013.

content: Commissioner Valerie Wynn of District 1 serves as the current Mayor Pro Tem of Macon-Bibb County. She was elected to the role by a 5-3 commission vote on January 6, 2026, replacing Seth Clark, who resigned from both his District 5 seat and the Mayor Pro Tem position on January 5, 2026 to run for lieutenant governor. The Mayor Pro Tem presides over commission meetings in the mayor's absence and performs the mayor's duties when the mayor cannot serve.

content: The 2024 Macon-Bibb County elections produced significant turnover on the commission: four of nine seats changed hands because longtime commissioners were term-limited. Elaine Lucas (District 3), Mallory Jones (District 4), Virgil Watkins (District 8), and Al Tillman (District 9) all reached their term limits. New commissioners Stanley Stewart, Joey Hulett, Donice Bryant, and Brendalyn Bailey replaced them.
hypothetical_questions: How many new commissioners joined in 2024?
Which commissioners were term-limited?
Was there big turnover on the commission?

content: Dr. Keith Moffett serves as Macon-Bibb County Manager, overseeing the day-to-day administration of 21 county departments with an approximate annual budget of $550 million. He was unanimously appointed by the commission in 2018 and is responsible for implementing the policies and directives of the mayor and commission. Julie Moore serves as assistant county manager.
hypothetical_questions: Who is the county manager?
Who is Keith Moffett?
What is the county budget?
How many county departments are there?

content: Macon-Bibb County is run by a mayor and nine commissioners — ten elected people who together make decisions for the whole county. Because Macon and Bibb County merged in 2014, this one group handles everything a city and a county would normally do separately. So your local government is more streamlined than most places, with one team in charge instead of two.
hypothetical_questions: How is the Macon-Bibb government structured?
How many commissioners are there?
Who runs Macon-Bibb County?
Is Macon a city or a county?
Why is Macon-Bibb one government?

content: Macon-Bibb County is split into 9 districts, numbered 1 through 9, and each district elects one commissioner to represent the people who live there. Your address decides which district you're in. You can look up your district at mvp.sos.ga.gov by typing in your address.
hypothetical_questions: How many commission districts are there?
How are commissioners elected?
What district am I in?
Are commissioners elected countywide?
How do I find my district?

content: The mayor is the person in charge of running Macon-Bibb County day to day — kind of like a CEO for the local government. The mayor sets priorities, runs the departments (like police, fire, and public works), proposes how the county should spend its money, and makes sure laws are carried out. The mayor is elected by voters across the whole county.
hypothetical_questions: What does the mayor do?
What are the mayor's powers?
What does a mayor do?
What is the mayor in charge of?

content: The mayor runs commission meetings but normally doesn't vote. The one exception is ties — if the nine commissioners split their vote (which is rare since there are 9 of them), the mayor can step in to break it. The mayor can also suggest new local laws, just like a commissioner can.
hypothetical_questions: Does the mayor vote on the commission?
Can the mayor vote?
Does the mayor break ties?
Is the mayor part of the commission?

content: A mayor's term is 4 years, and no one can serve more than 2 terms in a row — that's an 8-year maximum. After two terms, the mayor has to step aside. Mayor Lester Miller is in his second term now, so he won't be on the ballot in 2028.
hypothetical_questions: How long is the mayor's term?
Can the mayor serve three terms?
What are the mayor's term limits?
How many terms can the mayor serve?

content: To run for mayor of Macon-Bibb County, you need to be at least 27 years old, a U.S. citizen, a Georgia resident for at least 2 years, and a Macon-Bibb resident for at least 1 year before the election. You also have to be a registered voter here and keep living in the county while you serve.
hypothetical_questions: Who can run for mayor?
What are the requirements to be mayor?
How old do you have to be to run for mayor?
What do I need to run for mayor?

content: The mayor of Macon-Bibb County makes $100,000 a year and it's treated as a full-time job. The mayor can only get a raise in line with the average raise county employees get — so the mayor can't vote themselves a big pay bump. The mayor also gets reimbursed for reasonable work expenses.
hypothetical_questions: How much does the mayor make?
What is the mayor's salary?
Is the mayor a full-time job?
Does the mayor get a salary?

content: A commissioner's term is 4 years, and no one can serve more than 3 terms in a row — so the maximum is 12 years. After that they have to step aside. This is why four commissioners were replaced in 2024: they had served the limit.
hypothetical_questions: How long is a commissioner's term?
Can a commissioner serve four terms?
What are the term limits for commissioners?
How many terms can a commissioner serve?

content: To run for commissioner in Macon-Bibb County, you need to be at least 21 years old, a U.S. citizen, a Georgia resident for at least 2 years, and a resident of the specific district you want to represent for at least 1 year before the election. You also have to be a registered voter, and you have to keep living in your district the whole time you serve.
hypothetical_questions: Who can run for commissioner?
What are the requirements to be a commissioner?
How old do you have to be to run for commission?
Do commissioners have to live in their district?
What do I need to run for commission?

content: A commissioner makes $15,000 a year, and it's considered a part-time job. Commissioners can only get raises in line with what county employees get, and any raise only kicks in for the next batch of commissioners after the next election — so current commissioners can't vote themselves a pay bump.
hypothetical_questions: How much does a commissioner make?
What is a commissioner's salary?
Are commissioners full-time?
Do commissioners get paid?

content: The Mayor Pro Tem is one of the nine commissioners who's picked each January to fill in for the mayor when the mayor isn't available. The other commissioners choose among themselves each year. Right now it's Valerie Wynn (District 1), chosen in January 2026.
hypothetical_questions: How is the Mayor Pro Tem chosen?
When is the Mayor Pro Tem elected?
Who picks the Mayor Pro Tem?
What is a Mayor Pro Tem?

content: For the commission to hold an official meeting and do business, at least 6 of the 9 commissioners have to show up. That minimum number is called a quorum. If only 5 or fewer show up, no decisions can be made — they have to reschedule.
hypothetical_questions: What is a quorum for the commission?
How many commissioners make a quorum?
What is the quorum?
How many commissioners need to be present?
What's the minimum attendance?

content: To pass a new local law, spend money, or take any official action, at least 5 of the 9 commissioners have to vote yes. That's the normal threshold for almost everything the commission does — a simple majority of the full commission. Any commissioner can ask for a roll-call vote, which means each person's vote gets recorded by name.
hypothetical_questions: How does the commission vote?
How many votes are needed to pass an ordinance?
How many votes does the commission need?
What is the voting threshold on the commission?
How many commissioners need to vote yes?
How are local laws passed in Macon?

content: When the commission passes something, it goes to the mayor next. The mayor has 10 days to do one of three things: sign it (which makes it official right away), veto it (which stops it unless the commission fights back), or just sit on it. If the mayor does nothing for 10 days, it becomes law automatically at noon on day 10.
hypothetical_questions: Can the mayor veto an ordinance?
What is the mayor's veto power?
How long does the mayor have to veto?
What happens if the mayor doesn't sign an ordinance?
Does the mayor have to approve laws?

content: If the mayor vetoes something the commission passed, the commission can still push it through — but it takes 6 votes instead of the usual 5. That's called overriding a veto. The commission gets one or two chances at a meeting to gather those 6 votes. If they do, the law goes into effect even though the mayor said no.
hypothetical_questions: Can the commission override a veto?
How many votes are needed to override a veto?
What is a veto override?
How does the commission override the mayor?
Can commissioners go around the mayor?

content: When the commission passes the yearly budget, the mayor can veto specific line items instead of the whole thing — like rejecting just one department's spending while keeping everything else. The mayor can also lower a dollar amount instead of striking it completely. The commission can override these vetoes with 6 votes, same as any other veto.
hypothetical_questions: Does the mayor have a line-item veto?
Can the mayor veto part of a budget?
Can the mayor reduce appropriations?
Can the mayor change the budget?

content: The commission meets at least once a month, but usually twice: on the first and third Tuesdays, at 6:00 PM. Meetings are held in the Commission Chambers at 700 Poplar Street in downtown Macon, and anyone can attend. The schedule is set by the commission and can change, so it's worth checking maconbibb.us before going.
hypothetical_questions: How often does the commission meet?
When are commission meetings?
What day does the commission meet?
When is the next commission meeting?
Where are commission meetings?
source: Macon-Bibb County Charter, Section 19(b); Macon-Bibb County government

content: On top of regular meetings, the commission can hold extra meetings when something can't wait — called special meetings. These can be called by the mayor (for money or public-safety emergencies), the Mayor Pro Tem, or a majority of commissioners. Commissioners have to get 48 hours of notice, and the meeting can only cover the specific topic it was called for.
hypothetical_questions: Who can call a special commission meeting?
What is a special meeting?
How much notice is required for a special meeting?
Can the commission meet outside the regular schedule?

content: Every commission meeting is open to the public — you can just walk in and watch. Macon-Bibb also livestreams meetings on its Facebook page and posts recordings afterward at maconbibb.tv, so you can catch up even if you can't make it in person. This is your right under Georgia's Open Meetings law.
hypothetical_questions: Are commission meetings public?
Can I watch a commission meeting?
Are meetings livestreamed?
Can I attend commission meetings?
How can I watch commission meetings?

content: If something urgent comes up — a natural disaster, a public safety threat — the commission can skip the normal process and pass an emergency law on the spot. But there are limits: emergency laws can't raise taxes, give out franchises, change utility rates, or borrow money for more than 30 days. The commission also has to clearly say what the emergency is.
hypothetical_questions: Can the commission pass emergency ordinances?
What is an emergency ordinance?
Can laws be passed quickly?
What can't emergency ordinances do?

content: When a commissioner or mayor leaves office before their term ends — whether they resign, move away, or pass away — the county has to hold a special election to let voters pick someone to fill the rest of the term. That's what happened with District 5 in 2026 after Seth Clark resigned, and why Andrea Cooke was elected in April.
hypothetical_questions: What happens if a commissioner resigns?
How are vacancies filled?
What if the mayor resigns?
How do special elections work?
What if someone leaves office early?

content: If a commissioner's seat becomes vacant less than a year before the term would have ended anyway, the other commissioners can just appoint a replacement instead of holding an expensive special election. They have 20 days to do it. This only applies to commission seats — not the mayor.
hypothetical_questions: What if a vacancy happens near the end of a term?
Can the commission appoint someone to fill a seat?
When can vacancies be filled by appointment?

content: If the mayor leaves office early, the Mayor Pro Tem (currently Valerie Wynn) automatically takes over as acting mayor with all the same powers, until a new mayor is elected. If the vacancy happens in the last year of the term, the Mayor Pro Tem just finishes out the term — no special election needed for mayor.
hypothetical_questions: What happens if the mayor resigns?
Who becomes mayor if there's a vacancy?
Does the Mayor Pro Tem become mayor?

content: When the commission appoints someone to fill a vacant seat, it takes a majority of all the commissioners currently serving — not just those who show up that day. So if all nine seats are filled, 5 yes votes are needed. If one seat is already vacant, it would be 5 out of 8.
hypothetical_questions: How many votes are needed to appoint a replacement?
What is the appointment threshold?
How are vacancy appointments approved?

content: The commission has a lot of power over everyday life in Macon-Bibb. It passes the yearly budget, sets local laws, creates or gets rid of county departments, sets personnel policies, adjusts commission districts after the census, regulates public utilities, and handles trash collection. It can also take on any other power that state law allows — so the list above isn't exhaustive.
hypothetical_questions: What powers does the commission have?
What can the commission do?
Can the commission set the budget?
Can the commission create departments?
What does the commission control?

content: The county manager is the top professional administrator — kind of like a chief operating officer — who runs daily operations and coordinates all the departments. The mayor picks the county manager, but a majority of the commission has to approve the choice. Macon-Bibb's current county manager is Dr. Keith Moffett, appointed in 2018.
hypothetical_questions: How is the county manager appointed?
What does the county manager do?
Who is the county manager?
Why does the county have a manager?

content: The mayor picks several key positions, but the commission has to approve most of them: the county manager, the county attorney, the fire chief, the clerk of commission, the municipal court judge, and the heads of county departments. All of these people serve at the mayor's pleasure — meaning the mayor can replace them.
hypothetical_questions: Who does the mayor appoint?
How are department heads chosen?
Does the commission approve mayor's appointments?
Who appoints the fire chief?

content: Every year the county's books get checked by an independent outside accountant — that's called an audit. It happens automatically if the mayor and 5 commissioners agree, or if 6 commissioners agree without the mayor. The commission can also order extra audits of any specific office or department anytime.
hypothetical_questions: Does Macon-Bibb have an annual audit?
How does the audit work?
Who checks the county's money?
Can the commission order an audit?

content: The commission can formally investigate how any part of county government is working — a specific department, board, or agency. It takes the mayor plus 5 commissioners to start one, or 6 commissioners alone. The hearings have to be public under Georgia's Open Meetings law, so residents can watch.
hypothetical_questions: Can the commission investigate departments?
What investigative powers does the commission have?
Can the commission hold hearings?
Can commissioners look into problems?

content: The mayor has to hand the commission a proposed budget at least 6 weeks before the next fiscal year starts. The commission then reviews it, can make changes, and passes a final version before the new year begins. That 6-week window is the public's best chance to weigh in before spending decisions are locked.
hypothetical_questions: When is the budget submitted?
How does the budget process work?
Who prepares the budget?
When does the commission adopt the budget?
When can I give input on the budget?

content: The county charter — the document that spells out how Macon-Bibb's government works — can only be changed two ways: by a law passed by the Georgia state legislature, or by the county commission itself passing a change. This makes the charter harder to change than a regular local law, which protects the basic structure of the government.
hypothetical_questions: How is the charter amended?
Can the charter be changed?
Who changes the charter?
How do you change how the government works?

content: Macon-Bibb County, Georgia had a population of 157,346 in the 2020 U.S. Census. The most recent Census Bureau estimate places the figure at approximately 157,000. The county covers 255 square miles, and its demographics are approximately 54% Black or African American, 36% White, 4% Hispanic or Latino, and 2% Asian.
hypothetical_questions: What is the population of Macon?
How many people live in Macon-Bibb?
How big is Macon-Bibb County?
What are Macon's demographics?

content: Macon and Bibb County consolidated into a single unified government on January 1, 2014, after voters approved the merger in a July 31, 2012 referendum. Macon city voters approved with 57.8% and Bibb County voters with 56.7%. The consolidation merged the City of Macon, the City of Payne City, and Bibb County into one government called Macon-Bibb County. Four previous consolidation attempts had failed — in 1933, 1960, 1972, and 1976 — making the 2012 vote a historic turning point for Middle Georgia.
hypothetical_questions: When did Macon and Bibb County merge?
When did consolidation happen?
Why did Macon and Bibb County consolidate?
What was the 2012 referendum?

content: The consolidation of Macon and Bibb County means that one government provides both city and county services, including public safety, water and sewer, parks and recreation, planning and zoning, and code enforcement. The City of Payne City initially voted against consolidation but was dissolved by the Georgia General Assembly in 2015 and merged into Macon-Bibb County. Robert Reichert was the first mayor of the consolidated government (2014-2020), succeeded by Lester Miller in January 2020.
hypothetical_questions: What is a consolidated government?
What services does Macon-Bibb provide?
What happened to Payne City?
Who was the first mayor after consolidation?

content: The Macon-Bibb County Government Center is located at 700 Poplar Street, Macon, GA 31201. The main government phone number is (478) 751-7400, and the official website is maconbibb.us. Macon is the county seat of Bibb County, which is also the name of the consolidated government's legal entity for state-law purposes.
hypothetical_questions: Where is the Macon-Bibb government office?
What is the address of city hall?
How do I contact the government?
What is the county seat?

content: The Macon-Bibb County Commission holds regular meetings on the first and third Tuesdays of each month at 6:00 PM in the Commission Chambers at 700 Poplar Street. A Committee of the Whole meeting is held on the second Tuesday of each month at 9:00 AM. All meetings are open to the public, livestreamed on the county's Facebook page, and archived at maconbibb.tv.
hypothetical_questions: When does the commission meet?
When are commission meetings?
Where are commission meetings held?
Can I watch commission meetings online?

content: Meeting agendas and minutes for the Macon-Bibb County Commission are posted at maconbibbcoga.portal.civicclerk.com. Meeting videos are archived on YouTube and Vimeo at maconbibb.tv. All commission business except limited executive sessions is conducted in public under Georgia's Open Meetings Act.
hypothetical_questions: Where can I find commission agendas?
Are commission minutes public?
How do I see past commission meetings?

content: The official Macon-Bibb County government website is maconbibb.us. The Bibb County Board of Elections website is maconbibb.us/board-of-elections. The county also operates an open data portal called Macon Insights at maconinsights.maconbibb.us with public dashboards, maps, and data.
hypothetical_questions: What is the official Macon-Bibb website?
Where is the government website?
Is there an open data portal?

content: Macon-Bibb County uses SeeClickFix as its 311 system for reporting non-emergency issues such as potholes, code violations, and other service requests. The online services portal at maconbibb.us/online-services provides access to various government functions. A directory of department phone numbers is at maconbibb.us/helpful-phone-numbers.
hypothetical_questions: How do I report a pothole?
What is the 311 number for Macon?
How do I report a code violation?
How do I contact city services?

content: Bibb County is part of the Macon Judicial Circuit, which also includes Crawford and Peach Counties and is served by five Superior Court judges. The circuit's District Attorney's office can be reached at (478) 621-6427 with a website at maconjudicialcircuitda.com. Bibb County also falls within the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Georgia and the 11th Circuit Court of Appeals.
hypothetical_questions: What judicial circuit is Macon in?
What courts serve Macon?
Who is the District Attorney?
How many Superior Court judges are there?

content: The Bibb County School District serves approximately 21,000 students across 35 or more schools, including elementary, middle, high, and specialty programs. The superintendent is Dr. Dan A. Sims, who has served since July 2022. The school board consists of eight members serving four-year terms, and the district's website is bcsdk12.net.
hypothetical_questions: How many schools are in Bibb County?
Who is the schools superintendent?
How big is the Bibb County School District?
How many school board members are there?

content: The Macon-Bibb County Tax Commissioner's office is located at 188 Third Street and can be reached at (478) 621-6500, with a website at maconbibbtax.us. The Board of Assessors website is bibbassessors.com, and the Planning and Zoning Commission website is mbpz.org. These offices handle property tax billing, property assessments, and land use decisions.
hypothetical_questions: How do I pay my property taxes?
Who is the tax commissioner?
Where is the tax office?
How do I contact the assessor?

content: The most important voting resources for Macon-Bibb County are the Georgia My Voter Page at mvp.sos.ga.gov for personalized sample ballots and polling locations, the Bibb County Board of Elections at maconbibb.us/board-of-elections or (478) 621-6622, and the Georgia Secretary of State's site at sos.ga.gov. Voters can register online at registertovote.sos.ga.gov and request absentee ballots at securemyabsenteeballot.sos.ga.gov. All of these resources are free.
hypothetical_questions: Where can I find voting information?
How do I access my voter information?
What are the best voting resources?
source: Georgia Secretary of State; Bibb County Board of Elections

content: Macon-Bibb County is divided between U.S. Congressional Districts 2 and 12 following the court-ordered 2023 redistricting. The county is also covered by Georgia State Senate Districts 18 and 26 and State House Districts 142, 143, 144, and 145. Voters can find their exact state and federal districts by entering their address at legis.ga.gov/find-my-legislator or mvp.sos.ga.gov.
hypothetical_questions: What congressional district am I in?
What state senate district is Macon in?
What state house district is Macon in?
How do I find my legislators?
source: Georgia General Assembly; Georgia Secretary of State

content: A primary election is an early-round election where each political party picks who its candidates will be for the later general election. In Georgia, anyone registered can vote in the primary, but you have to pick one party's ballot for that day. The general election — where all voters pick between the parties' chosen candidates — comes later, in November.
hypothetical_questions: What is a primary?
What does primary mean?
What is a primary election?
Why is there a primary?
What's the difference between primary and general?

content: A runoff is a second election held when no one in a race gets more than 50% of the vote. Only the top two candidates move on, and voters pick between them. Georgia runoffs happen about a month after the main election. For the May 19, 2026 primary, runoffs (if needed) are June 16, 2026.
hypothetical_questions: What is a runoff?
What does runoff mean?
Why are runoffs held?
When is the runoff?

content: Nonpartisan means a race where candidates don't run as Democrats or Republicans — they just run as themselves. Judge races, school board races, and Macon-Bibb city commission races are all nonpartisan. Since there are no party primaries for these races, the whole thing is decided on Election Day in May (or June if there's a runoff).
hypothetical_questions: What is nonpartisan?
What does nonpartisan mean?
Why are some races nonpartisan?
Which races are nonpartisan in Macon?

content: The general election is the big Election Day in November where voters across Georgia pick the winners for state and federal offices. For 2026, it's on Tuesday, November 3. By then, each party has already picked its candidates in the May primary, so November is when everyone votes between them.
hypothetical_questions: What is the general election?
When is the general election?
What's the difference between primary and general?
When is Election Day?

content: An ordinance is just a fancy word for a local law passed by the county commission. Things like setting the county budget, changing zoning rules, or creating new local rules are all done by passing ordinances. To pass one in Macon-Bibb, at least 5 of the 9 commissioners have to vote yes.
hypothetical_questions: What is an ordinance?
What does ordinance mean?
What is a local law?
How do local laws get made?

content: A consolidated government is one unified local government that does the job of both a city and a county at once. Macon and Bibb County merged into one government in 2014 — before that, Macon had its own city hall and Bibb County had a separate county commission. Now one mayor and nine commissioners handle everything together. Only a few places in Georgia are set up this way.
hypothetical_questions: What is a consolidated government?
What does consolidated mean?
Why is Macon-Bibb one government?
How is Macon-Bibb different from other counties?

content: The commission is the group of nine elected people who make decisions for Macon-Bibb County. Each commissioner represents one of the nine districts in the county. Together with the mayor, they pass laws, approve the budget, and set policy. Think of them as the county's legislature or city council — they're the people you elect to represent your neighborhood.
hypothetical_questions: What is the commission?
What does the commission do?
Who are the commissioners?
What's the difference between the mayor and commissioners?

content: The Mayor Pro Tem is the commissioner who steps in when the mayor can't be there — running meetings or handling duties in the mayor's absence. It's not a separate elected role; the nine commissioners pick one of their own every January. If the mayor leaves office early, the Mayor Pro Tem automatically takes over until a new mayor is elected.
hypothetical_questions: What is a Mayor Pro Tem?
What does Mayor Pro Tem mean?
What does the Mayor Pro Tem do?
How is Mayor Pro Tem different from Mayor?

content: To find out which commissioner represents you, go to mvp.sos.ga.gov and enter your home address — it'll tell you your district number. You can also look up all nine commission districts and their current members at peachtracker.vercel.app/commission. Every address in Macon-Bibb falls into exactly one of the nine districts, so you have one specific commissioner who is your representative.
hypothetical_questions: Who is my commissioner?
Who represents me?
How do I find my commissioner?
Which commissioner is mine?
Which district am I in?

content: If you want to contact your commissioner about a problem in your neighborhood or an issue you care about, the best starting point is maconbibb.us/commissioners — each commissioner has a page with their contact info. You can also show up and speak at a commission meeting (first and third Tuesdays, 6 PM, 700 Poplar Street), or call the main county number at (478) 751-7400 to be directed. For things like potholes or code issues, it's usually faster to use the county's 311 system (SeeClickFix) than to go through a commissioner.
hypothetical_questions: How do I contact my commissioner?
How do I reach my commissioner?
How do I complain to the county?
How do I get the county to fix something?
Can I talk to my commissioner?

content: Anyone can speak at a Macon-Bibb commission meeting during the public comment period. Meetings are the first and third Tuesdays of each month at 6:00 PM at 700 Poplar Street, and public comment usually happens near the start. You don't need to sign up in advance — just show up and wait your turn. You typically get 3 minutes to speak. Meeting agendas are posted in advance at maconbibb.us so you know what topics are on the table.
hypothetical_questions: Can I speak at a commission meeting?
How do I give public comment?
How can I tell the commission what I think?
How do I participate in a meeting?
Can I attend a commission meeting?

content: If you've never voted before, here's the short version: first, make sure you're registered (deadline for the May 19 primary is April 20). You can register online at registertovote.sos.ga.gov in about five minutes if you have a Georgia driver's license. Once you're registered, you have three ways to vote: early voting (April 27 – May 15, any county location), Election Day (May 19, your assigned precinct only), or absentee by mail (request by May 8). You don't have to pick a political party to register in Georgia.
hypothetical_questions: How do I vote for the first time?
I've never voted, what do I do?
How does voting work?
What do I need to vote?
Am I too late to register?

content: Your ballot for May 19, 2026 depends on where you live in Bibb County and which party's primary you pick (Democratic, Republican, or nonpartisan-only). The easiest way to see exactly what's on your ballot is the Georgia My Voter Page at mvp.sos.ga.gov — just enter your address and it'll show you every race you'll vote in, personalized to you. You'll typically see races for governor, U.S. senator, state legislature, judges, school board, and the Macon Water Authority.
hypothetical_questions: What's on my ballot?
What am I voting on?
What races are on the ballot?
What will I vote for?
How do I see my ballot?

content: Local elections often matter more for your daily life than national ones — your commissioner decides things like which roads get fixed, how police are funded, what gets built in your neighborhood, and how much you pay in property taxes. Turnout for local elections is also way lower than for presidential elections, which means your vote counts more. Some Macon-Bibb races in recent years have been decided by a handful of votes.
hypothetical_questions: Why should I vote in local elections?
Does my vote matter?
Why does local politics matter?
What do commissioners actually do for me?

content: To check if you're registered to vote in Bibb County, go to mvp.sos.ga.gov and enter your name and date of birth. It'll confirm your registration status, show where your polling place is, and let you see a sample of your ballot. If you're not registered (or your info is out of date), you can fix it at registertovote.sos.ga.gov — the deadline for the May 19 primary is April 20, 2026.
hypothetical_questions: How do I check if I'm registered?
Am I registered to vote?
How do I know if I can vote?
Is my registration current?
